title: Uganda Evacuation Routes & Transport (DEMO - Historical)
description: Escape route planning during the 1972 Asian expulsion. Approximately 80,000 people forced to leave in 90 days.
items: Entebbe Airport - primary but dangerous route. Military presence heavy. Luggage inspections invasive and confiscatory.
Overland to Kenya via Jinja/Tororo - risky but used by those denied air travel. Soldiers at border demanded bribes or seized goods.
Check visa requirements BEFORE traveling - UK accepted British passport holders but with restrictions. India, Pakistan, Canada offered refuge.
Book flights early - demand far exceeded capacity. Airlines added emergency flights but at inflated prices.
Expect confiscation at airport - anything of value was at risk: jewelry, cash above limit, cameras, electronics
Cavity searches were common - humiliating and traumatic, particularly for women. No privacy or dignity.
Military could detain indefinitely without cause - some were held for weeks, tortured, or killed
Arrive at airport early - but not too early. Waiting areas were chaotic, dangerous, and uncomfortable.
Do NOT resist or argue with soldiers - compliance was survival. Pride was a luxury no one could afford.
Transit countries - some families stopped in Kenya or Tanzania temporarily. This required additional visas and funds.
Refugee camps - some ended up in camps in Kenya or Italy before final resettlement. Conditions were harsh.
Keep ticket and passport accessible but secure - losing either meant you weren't leaving
